findings.finding: The lesson carefully disambiguates the overloaded term 'secure' (node-level provisional state vs. capital-S Secure entry-station band) but does not note that 'fragile' is also overloaded: syllabus section 7 uses 'recall is fragile' informally within the Acquiring state definition, while this lesson defines 'fragile knowledge' as a formal operational node state. A learner reading both documents could conflate lesson-fragile with syllabus-Acquiring, missing that lesson-fragile can also correspond to syllabus-Practising.
findings.resolution: Add a brief parenthetical to the crosswalk noting that the syllabus's informal use of 'fragile' within the A-state description is not equivalent to this lesson's operational fragile state, which spans both A and P routing.

findings.finding: The three-vocabulary crosswalk (node-level operational states, entry-station bands, longitudinal N/A/P/M/R/T states) is well-constructed and directly verified against the syllabus. It correctly identifies the grain-size differences, prevents false synonymy, and makes the overloading of 'secure' explicit. This disambiguation is critical for preventing a learner from equating a station-level Secure result with the richer node-level evidence pattern this lesson requires.

This claim ('Confidence must be compared with performance evidence') cites only source-premed-syllabus. The syllabus explicitly supports the high-confidence-error direction (section 7: 'A high-confidence incorrect response triggers misconception remediation') and establishes bidirectional calibration as a course requirement (section 6.2: 'confidence judgments track actual performance'; PLO-18: 'confidence calibration'). The low-confidence-accurate scenario taught in the lesson is a logically sound inference from this framework. The 'medium-high' confidence level appropriately reflects single-source support for an operational claim that the lesson does not present as a general psychological finding.

The 25-point assessment with deterministic scoring is internally consistent: rubric criterion maxPoints sum exactly to item points for all four items, the selected-response scoring logic correctly enumerates all option combinations, and the mastery gate (20/25 aggregate, 6/10 per open response, no-zero criterion on open-response rubrics) prevents a learner from demonstrating only classification or only transfer while compensating with the other.

limitations: Reviewer cannot empirically test whether the operational three-state model produces reliable classifications when applied by actual Premed learners; the lesson explicitly marks this as pending learner evaluation.

Reviewer verified claims only against the four pinned repository-owned sources; no external empirical literature on diagnostic classification reliability was independently consulted beyond what the evidence synthesis already reports.
Reviewer cannot execute the lesson in a browser or verify that the assessment interface correctly implements the described scoring logic at runtime.
